content.text: Test Strategy Aligned to Release Trains

A release train is a time-boxed bundle of changes that ships together on a fixed cadence — weekly, biweekly, or monthly. For GenAI features, aligning the test strategy to the train has a specific implication: prompt versions, model versions, and eval baselines must be frozen at the moment the train is cut, and the full eval suite must pass against that frozen state before the train is allowed to deploy. If the eval fails at train cut, the train is held — the fix ships on the next train.

This sub-subtopic covers how to structure tests so each layer runs at the right point in the train timeline, how to freeze AI-specific artifacts at cut time, and how to automate the train gate so no human has to remember which tests to run.

attrs.alt: Release train timeline: unit/snapshot on commit, tier-1 evals per PR, full eval + integration at train cut, E2E on staging, smoke post-deploy

content.text: The Release Train Mental Model for GenAI

In a standard software release train, the only artifacts that matter are code and config. In a GenAI application, three additional artifacts must be versioned and frozen with the train: prompts, model pins, and eval baselines. Without freezing these, a model upgrade that lands after train cut will change the AI output but the eval suite will have run against the old model — the train passes a test it has not actually taken.
Artifact
Why It Matters
How to Freeze
Prompt version
Prompt changes shift output quality — must be tested as a unit with the code that calls them
SHA-pin the prompt file in the train manifest; reject any post-cut prompt edits
Model version
Model upgrades can change response style, token usage, and safety behaviour
Pin the full model ID (e.g. gpt-4o-2024-11-20) in the train's config file
Eval baseline
Each train's quality floor is specific to its prompt and model version
Commit baselines/train-YYYY-QN.json and reference it in the CI eval gate
Test corpus
The retrieval dataset used in integration tests must match what the train will serve
Tag the corpus version in the train manifest; integration tests load that tag
The Train Manifest

A train manifest is a small YAML file committed at train cut time that records all frozen AI-specific artifacts. The CI gate reads this file rather than inferring artifact versions from environment variables, making the test run reproducible:

# train-manifests/train-2025-Q3-w28.yaml
# Cut: 2025-07-07T09:00:00Z by @release-bot
# To hold the train: set status: held and push a commit with the reason.

train_id: "2025-Q3-w28"
cut_at: "2025-07-07T09:00:00Z"
status: "testing"   # values: testing | held | shipped | rolled-back

# Frozen AI artifacts
ai_artifacts:
  prompt_versions:
    summarise: "prompts/summarise/v3.2.txt"
    summarise_sha: "a3f8c2d1..."
    rerank:   "prompts/rerank/v1.7.txt"
    rerank_sha: "b9e4f01a..."

  model_pins:
    primary_llm:   "gpt-4o-2024-11-20"
    judge_llm:     "gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18"
    embedding_model: "text-embedding-3-small"

  eval_baseline: "baselines/train-2025-Q3-w28.json"
  test_corpus_tag: "corpus-v4.1"

rollback_target: "train-2025-Q3-w26"  # prior shipped train
Aligning Prompt Versions with the Train

At train cut, a script writes the frozen prompt SHA into the train manifest and into the application config file that ships with the train. Any commit that changes a prompt file after cut is flagged and must wait for the next train:

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# scripts/cut_train.py — generate the train manifest at cut time
"""
Run by the release bot at train cut time:
  python3 scripts/cut_train.py --train-id 2025-Q3-w28 --rollback train-2025-Q3-w26
"""
import argparse, hashlib, json, subprocess, sys, time
from pathlib import Path
import yaml

PROMPT_FILES = {
    "summarise": "prompts/summarise/v3.2.txt",
    "rerank":    "prompts/rerank/v1.7.txt",
}

MODEL_PINS = {
    "primary_llm":    "gpt-4o-2024-11-20",
    "judge_llm":      "gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18",
    "embedding_model": "text-embedding-3-small",
}

def sha_of(path: str) -> str:
    content = Path(path).read_bytes()
    return hashlib.sha256(content).hexdigest()[:12]

def latest_eval_baseline(train_id: str) -> str:
    return f"baselines/{train_id}.json"

def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--train-id", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--rollback", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--corpus-tag", default="corpus-v4.1")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    prompt_versions = {}
    for name, path in PROMPT_FILES.items():
        prompt_versions[name] = path
        prompt_versions[f"{name}_sha"] = sha_of(path)

    manifest = {
        "train_id": args.train_id,
        "cut_at": time.strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ", time.gmtime()),
        "status": "testing",
        "ai_artifacts": {
            "prompt_versions": prompt_versions,
            "model_pins": MODEL_PINS,
            "eval_baseline": latest_eval_baseline(args.train_id),
            "test_corpus_tag": args.corpus_tag,
        },
        "rollback_target": args.rollback,
    }

    out_path = Path(f"train-manifests/{args.train_id}.yaml")
    out_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    out_path.write_text(yaml.dump(manifest, sort_keys=False))
    print(f"Train manifest written: {out_path}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Automating the Train Gate

The train gate CI job reads the manifest, runs the full eval suite with the frozen model pin and baseline, and either marks the train as ready or holds it. The gate runs once at cut — not on every push — so it is a deliberate, expensive check rather than a fast-feedback loop:

# .github/workflows/train-gate.yml
# Triggered by the release bot when a train-manifests/*.yaml is committed.
name: Train Gate
on:
  push:
    paths:
      - "train-manifests/*.yaml"

jobs:
  read-manifest:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      train_id:    ${{ steps.parse.outputs.train_id }}
      baseline:    ${{ steps.parse.outputs.baseline }}
      model_pin:   ${{ steps.parse.outputs.model_pin }}
      corpus_tag:  ${{ steps.parse.outputs.corpus_tag }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Parse manifest
        id: parse
        run: |
          MANIFEST=$(ls train-manifests/*.yaml | tail -1)
          echo "train_id=$(yq .train_id $MANIFEST)" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          echo "baseline=$(yq .ai_artifacts.eval_baseline $MANIFEST)" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          echo "model_pin=$(yq '.ai_artifacts.model_pins.primary_llm' $MANIFEST)" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          echo "corpus_tag=$(yq .ai_artifacts.test_corpus_tag $MANIFEST)" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

  full-eval:
    needs: read-manifest
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    env:
      TIER2_EVALS: "true"
      OPENAI_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.EVAL_OPENAI_API_KEY }}
      EVAL_MODEL: ${{ needs.read-manifest.outputs.model_pin }}
      EVAL_BASELINE: ${{ needs.read-manifest.outputs.baseline }}
      TEST_CORPUS_TAG: ${{ needs.read-manifest.outputs.corpus_tag }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: "3.12"}
      - run: pip install -e ".[dev]"
      - name: Run full eval suite against frozen artifacts
        run: pytest tests/evals/ -q --timeout=600
      - name: Mark train as tested
        if: success()
        run: |
          python3 scripts/update_train_status.py \
            --train-id ${{ needs.read-manifest.outputs.train_id }} \
            --status ready-for-integration
      - name: Hold train on eval failure
        if: failure()
        run: |
          python3 scripts/update_train_status.py \
            --train-id ${{ needs.read-manifest.outputs.train_id }} \
            --status held \
            --reason "Full eval suite failed — see job logs"

  integration:
    needs: [read-manifest, full-eval]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    env:
      INTEGRATION_TEST_ENV: "true"
      OPENAI_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.PROD_OPENAI_API_KEY }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: "3.12"}
      - run: pip install -e ".[dev]"
      - run: pytest tests/integration/ -q --timeout=120
Versioning Eval Baselines per Train

Each train carries its own eval baseline — the minimum acceptable quality scores for the prompt and model versions it ships. A baseline from a previous train is not valid for the current train because it may have been produced with a different prompt or model. The baseline promotion workflow produces a new
baselines/train-YYYY-QN-wNN.json
file that the train gate references:

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# scripts/promote_eval_baseline.py
"""
Run during the pre-cut window to produce a new eval baseline
that the upcoming train gate will use as its quality floor.

Usage:
  python3 scripts/promote_eval_baseline.py \
    --train-id 2025-Q3-w28 \
    --prior-baseline baselines/train-2025-Q3-w26.json
"""
import argparse, asyncio, json
from pathlib import Path
from evals.runner import run_full_eval_suite  # runs all eval cases, returns scores dict
from evals.judge import summarise_scores

SCORE_FLOOR = {
    "overall": 0.78,       # must stay at or above prior overall score
    "factual_accuracy": 0.82,
    "no_hallucination": 0.90,
    "format_compliance": 0.95,
}

async def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--train-id", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--prior-baseline", required=True)
    args = parser.parse_args()

    prior = json.loads(Path(args.prior_baseline).read_text())
    print(f"Prior baseline: {args.prior_baseline}")
    print(f"Prior overall score: {prior['summary']['overall']:.3f}")

    print("Running full eval suite against current code...")
    raw_scores = await run_full_eval_suite()
    summary = summarise_scores(raw_scores)

    # Enforce floor: new baseline must not drop below floor OR prior score
    failures = []
    for metric, floor in SCORE_FLOOR.items():
        current = summary.get(metric, 0.0)
        prior_score = prior.get("summary", {}).get(metric, floor)
        if current < floor:
            failures.append(f"{metric}: {current:.3f} < absolute floor {floor:.3f}")
        if current < prior_score - 0.02:  # 2% slip tolerance
            failures.append(f"{metric}: {current:.3f} dropped >2% below prior {prior_score:.3f}")

    if failures:
        print("BASELINE PROMOTION BLOCKED:")
        for f in failures:
            print(f"  ✗ {f}")
        return 1

    baseline = {
        "train_id": args.train_id,
        "summary": summary,
        "per_case": raw_scores,
        "promoted_from": args.prior_baseline,
    }
    out_path = Path(f"baselines/{args.train_id}.json")
    out_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    out_path.write_text(json.dumps(baseline, indent=2))
    print(f"✓ New baseline written: {out_path}")
    print(f"  overall: {summary['overall']:.3f}")
    return 0

if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(asyncio.run(main()))
Rollback Testing: Verifying the Escape Hatch

A train must be rollback-testable before it ships. A rollback test confirms that the prior train's code, prompt versions, and model pin can be restored in under five minutes, and that the eval suite still passes against the prior baseline after rollback. This is tested in staging as part of the staging E2E gate:

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# scripts/test_rollback.py — simulate rollback to prior train and verify evals pass
"""
Run during the staging gate:
  python3 scripts/test_rollback.py --current-manifest train-manifests/2025-Q3-w28.yaml
"""
import argparse, asyncio, json, subprocess, sys
from pathlib import Path
import yaml

async def run_eval_with_manifest(manifest: dict) -> dict:
    """Run eval suite using model + prompt + baseline from manifest."""
    from evals.runner import run_full_eval_suite
    from evals.judge import summarise_scores
    model = manifest["ai_artifacts"]["model_pins"]["primary_llm"]
    baseline_path = manifest["ai_artifacts"]["eval_baseline"]
    raw = await run_full_eval_suite(model_override=model)
    return summarise_scores(raw), json.loads(Path(baseline_path).read_text())

async def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--current-manifest", required=True)
    args = parser.parse_args()

    current = yaml.safe_load(Path(args.current_manifest).read_text())
    rollback_id = current["rollback_target"]
    rollback_manifest_path = Path(f"train-manifests/{rollback_id}.yaml")
    if not rollback_manifest_path.exists():
        print(f"ERROR: Rollback manifest not found: {rollback_manifest_path}")
        sys.exit(1)

    rollback = yaml.safe_load(rollback_manifest_path.read_text())
    print(f"Simulating rollback to: {rollback_id}")

    scores, baseline = await run_eval_with_manifest(rollback)

    failures = []
    for metric, prior_score in baseline.get("summary", {}).items():
        if isinstance(prior_score, float) and scores.get(metric, 0) < prior_score - 0.05:
            failures.append(f"{metric}: {scores[metric]:.3f} < baseline {prior_score:.3f}")

    if failures:
        print("ROLLBACK TEST FAILED — prior train cannot be safely restored:")
        for f in failures:
            print(f"  ✗ {f}")
        sys.exit(1)

    print(f"✓ Rollback test passed — {rollback_id} is a valid escape hatch")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())
Detecting Prompt Regressions at Train Cut

A prompt regression is a change that reduces output quality — not because of a code bug, but because the prompt text changed in a way that shifts model behaviour. The train gate catches this by comparing the eval scores produced at cut time against the prior train's baseline. If scores drop by more than the configured tolerance, the cut is rejected:

# evals/regression_gate.py — used by the train gate CI job
from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass
import json
from pathlib import Path

TOLERANCE = 0.03  # 3% drop permitted; more than this blocks the train

@dataclass
class RegressionResult:
    passed: bool
    regressions: list[str]
    improvements: list[str]

def check_regression(
    current_scores: dict[str, float],
    baseline_path: str,
) -> RegressionResult:
    baseline = json.loads(Path(baseline_path).read_text())
    prior = baseline.get("summary", {})

    regressions = []
    improvements = []

    for metric, current in current_scores.items():
        prior_score = prior.get(metric)
        if prior_score is None:
            continue  # new metric — not a regression
        delta = current - prior_score
        if delta < -TOLERANCE:
            regressions.append(
                f"{metric}: {current:.3f} (was {prior_score:.3f}, Δ{delta:+.3f})"
            )
        elif delta > TOLERANCE:
            improvements.append(
                f"{metric}: {current:.3f} (was {prior_score:.3f}, Δ{delta:+.3f})"
            )

    return RegressionResult(
        passed=len(regressions) == 0,
        regressions=regressions,
        improvements=improvements,
    )


# Usage in CI:
# result = check_regression(scores, manifest["ai_artifacts"]["eval_baseline"])
# if not result.passed:
#     for r in result.regressions:
#         print(f"REGRESSION: {r}")
#     sys.exit(1)
Common Train Gate Failure Modes
Most train gate failures fall into four categories. Knowing the category helps the on-call engineer route the fix to the right team without a lengthy triage:
Failure Mode
Symptom
Cause
Mitigation
Prompt regression
Eval score drops > tolerance on one or more metrics
Prompt text changed in a way that shifts model behaviour
Diff the prompt SHA vs prior train; revert the prompt change or tune it
Model upgrade mismatch
Eval passes but response style diverges from baseline
Model pin was updated without re-running baseline promotion
Re-run promote_eval_baseline.py with the new model pin
Corpus drift
Citation tests fail; retrieved chunks reference unexpected docs
Test corpus tag in manifest differs from the index in the test environment
Re-tag the corpus or update the manifest to match the available corpus version
Flaky judge model
Eval score varies by ±0.05 across two identical runs
Judge model is itself non-deterministic at default temperature
Run judge at temperature 0; use majority-vote across 3 judge calls
Summary
Aligning a test strategy to a release train requires three disciplines:
freezing AI artifacts at cut time
(prompt SHA, model pin, eval baseline, corpus tag),
running expensive tests at the right moment
(full eval and integration only at cut, not on every push), and
holding the train
rather than shipping with a failing eval. The train manifest is the source of truth — every test job reads from it rather than from environment variables or implicit defaults.

The payoff is a reproducible audit trail: for any shipped train you can re-run the exact eval suite with the exact model and prompt versions that were tested, and get the same result. This is what makes it possible to distinguish a quality regression introduced in the current train from one that was already present in the prior train — a distinction that matters when investigating a production incident.
